content.full_text:
August 19, 1993


Stephanie A. Atigh
City Attorney
City of Salinas
Office of the City Attorney
200 Lincoln Avenue
Salinas, CA  93901

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-295

Dear Ms. Atigh:

You have requested advice on behalf of Mr. Roberto Ocampo, a
councilmember of the City of Salinas, regarding his duties and
obligations under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTION

Under the Act, may Mr. Ocampo participate in decisions
regarding CHISPA, a non-profit corporation, which is a source of
income to his business?

CONCLUSION

Mr. Ocampo may not participate in any decisions which will
have a material financial effect on CHISPA or on Spark
Investments.

FACTS

Mr. Ocampo is a member of both the city council of the City
of Salinas and the Salinas Redevelopment Agency. He and his wife
own a beauty salon in Salinas. The beauty salon received a
relocation benefit in the amount of $11,015.46 from CHISPA, a tax
exempt non-profit corporation located in Salinas. The first
installment of $6256.96 was received on May 1, 1992, and the
second installment of $4758.50 was received on October 15, 1992.

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-93-295
Page 2


CHISPA's purpose is to develop low income housing and
commercial/retail projects. CHISPA receives federally funded
Community Development Block Grant monies administered by the City
of Salinas. CHISPA is the general partner of Portales de la Paz
dba La Paz Partners, which developed a commercial/retail project
(Portales de Alisal) in Salinas for which the City of Salinas and
Salinas Redevelopment Agency have guaranteed a state loan.

In 1985, Mr. and Mrs. Ocampo invested $5,000 in Spark
Investments, which is a limited partnership located in Salinas.
Spark Investments is the owner of a commercial building, which is
leased and used by CHISPA as its principal office building.
Mr. Ocampo has never received any return or interest on this
investment; however, he has received federal and state tax credits
since 1987. The tax credits ranged from $2,452 in 1987 to $939.00
in 1992.

Mrs. Ocampo leases space for the beauty shop on a month-to-
month tenancy. The beauty shop is within 300 feet of the Portales
de la Paz Project (CHISPA as general partner) and within 2,500
feet of the Roosevelt low income housing project proposed by
CHISPA.

CHISPA regularly asks the city council to make decisions
regarding various low income housing projects. In particular, the
city council will be asked to approve a general plan amendment, a
rezone and a planned unit development permit for CHISPA's
Roosevelt housing project.

In addition, the city council and the Salinas Redevelopment
Agency is asked by Portales de la Paz to make decisions regarding
the Portales Project. The city council also is asked annually to
award Community Development Block Grants (CDBG) funds for CHISPA
for various low income housing project predevelopment costs,
including personnel and non-personnel expenses. CHISPA has in the
past submitted applications for CDBG funds for three low income
housing projects, including Roosevelt and the Portales commercial
project. The city council will be asked to make annual grants of
federal HOME funds (for low income permanent housing) to CHISPA
for the housing subsidies for the Roosevelt housing project.
CHISPA has also requested from the city council Salinas Housing
Trust Fund monies for its various low income housing projects.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participate in making, or using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or
has reason to know the official has a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a governmental deci-
sion within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably

File No. A-93-295
Page 3


foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial ef-
fect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on
the official, or on a member of the official's immediate family,
or on:

     (a)  Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (b)  Any real property in which the public official has a
direct or indirect interest worth one thousand dollars
($1,000) or more.

     (c)  Any source of income, other than gifts and other
than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business on terms available to the public without
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dol-
lars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the
time when the decision is made.

     (d)  Any business entity in which the public official is
a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management.

                              Section 87103(a)-(d).

     According to the facts presented, Mr. Ocampo has an
investment interest in a beauty shop, presumably worth more than
$1,000, and a $5,000 investment in Spark Investments, a limited
partnership. 2  (Section 87103(a).)  In addition, the beauty shop
and CHISPA are a source of income to him.  (Section 87103(c).)

     Pursuant to Section 82030(a), income of an individual also
includes "a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or
trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly
or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater."  Thus, the
clients of the beauty shop of $250 or more are a source of income
to Mr. Ocampo.

     You also indicate in your letter that Mr. and Mrs. Ocampo
rent property for the beauty salon on a month-to-month tenancy.
Pursuant to Regulation 18233, a periodic tenancy of one month or
less does not constitute a "leasehold interest" in real property.
Therefore, despite the proximity of the proposed projects to the

_______________________
2  Pursuant to the Nord Opinion ((1983) 8 FPPC Ops. 6, 11), a
limited partner has an investment interest in the two controlling
general partners.  We have no facts regarding Spark Investments.
However, if there are only two controlling general partners, Mr.
Ocampo must also disqualify himself from any decision which would
have a material financial effect on these partners.

File No. A-93-295
Page 4


beauty salon, Mr. Ocampo does not have an interest in real
property which may be affected by the decisions.

However, Councilmember Ocampo may not make, participate in
making, or attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision if the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect, distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally, on the beauty shop, Spark
Investments, CHISPA, or on any client that has been a source of
income to him of more than $250 within the past twelve months.3

Generally, an effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if
there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty
is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibl-
ity, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 198.)

The Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine
whether the effect of a decision is material, depending on the
specific circumstances of each decision. CHISPA, a nonprofit
corporation, has been a source of income to Mr. Ocampo within the
last twelve months. Therefore, if CHISPA is directly involved in the
decisions regarding the general plan amendment, the rezone and
the planned unit development permit for CHISPA's Roosevelt housing
project, Mr. Ocampo may not participate. (Regulation 18702.(a)
and (b).)

If CHISPA is indirectly involved in a decision, you must
determine under Regulation 18702.5 if the effect of the decision
will have a material financial effect on CHISPA. Please consult
Regulation 18702.5 (copy enclosed) to determine which dollar
threshold applies to CHISPA and if the effect will be material
pursuant to the applicable subsection. If there will be a
material financial effect on CHISPA, Mr. Ocampo may not
participate in the decision.

You have also mentioned several forthcoming decisions
regarding CHISPA. One decision involves the Portales Project, of
which CHISPA is the general partner. Other decisions involve
granting CDBG funds, HOME funds and Salinas Housing Trust Fund
monies to CHISPA for its various low income housing projects.
We do not have any facts pertaining to any of these decisions.
Therefore, you must analyze each decision under either
Regulation 18702.1 or 18702.5 to determine if there will be a
material financial effect on CHISPA. If there is, Mr. Ocampo may
not participate in the decision.


3          Disqualification means that the official cannot participate
in discussions, vote on a matter, or otherwise try to influence
members of the official's own agency or an agency appointed by or
subject to the budgetary control of the official's agency.

File No. A-93-295
Page 5


     Mr. Ocampo also has an investment interest in Spark
Investments which owns and leases a commercial building to CHISPA.
If any of the decisions regarding CHISPA will indirectly have a
material financial effect on Spark Investments, as determined by
Regulation 18702.2, Mr. Ocampo may not participate in the
decision.

     I trust this answers your question.


                                        Sincerely,

                                        Wayne Ordos
                                        Executive Director


                                        By: [illegible]
                                            Jim Stecher
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

WO:JS:ak

Enclosure
